rows.text: My y-axis actually downplays the disagreement: people who think AGI can't compress a century of progress into a few years *also* tend to think a century of progress won't reach sci-fi tech. A y-axis like energy production would show different ceilings, not just different slopes.

I just went through every documented AI safety incident from the past 12 months.

Unpopular opinion: AGIs ought to care enough about being trustworthy that they would not act deceptively even in a game. If this means they flatly refuse to play games like Mafia, that’s not over-refusal. I myself refuse to play Mafia, or attend surprise parties. cc 
@AmandaAskell
you don't attend surprise parties?

I prefer to be the sort of person who reliably avoids deceiving innocent people, and I believe the normalization of deception as a form of “fun” through surprise parties and games is a cultural practice that our eventual CEV will probably find abhorrent. Like ethnic jokes.

There are still a lot of journalists and commentators that I follow who think AI is nothing of much significance—still just a mildly fancy auto complete machine that hallucinates half the time and can’t even think.

Not all increases in AI inference cost are "inference scaling." If 2x inference cost is used for a task that takes a human 2x longer this just means larger tasks cost proportionally more.

The key question is cost *as a fraction of human cost*.

Reaching AGI won't be beating a benchmark. It will be the end of the human-AI gap. Benchmarks are simply a way to estimate the current gap, which is why we need to continually release new benchmarks (focused on the remaining gap). Benchmarking is a process, not a fixed point.

In the age of reasoning, we are making models that can solve complex, multi-step problems.
